tests.condition: Rule-out test in low / intermediate pre-test probability

citation.reference: Patel P, et al. Systematic review and meta-analysis of test accuracy for the diagnosis of suspected pulmonary embolism. Blood Adv. 2020;4(18):4296-4311 (61 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: The pooled estimates for D-dimer sensitivity and specificity were 0.97 (95% confidence interval [CI], 0.96-0.98) and 0.41 (95% CI, 0.36-0.46) respectively.
citation.context: Quantitative D-dimer; patients with suspected first/recurrent PE. Values vary by assay (ELISA vs latex vs whole-blood) and increase in specificity with age-adjusted thresholds.
citation.notes: High sensitivity / low specificity makes a NEGATIVE result a strong rule-out (low LR-). DOI/PMID must be re-verified by faculty before teaching use.

tests.condition: Confirmatory imaging, typically after a positive D-dimer or high pre-test probability

citation.reference: Patel P, et al. Systematic review and meta-analysis of test accuracy for the diagnosis of suspected pulmonary embolism. Blood Adv. 2020;4(18):4296-4311 (61 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: CTPA sensitivity and specificity were 0.94 (95% CI, 0.89-0.97) and 0.98 (95% CI, 0.97-0.99), respectively.
citation.context: CT pulmonary angiography in suspected PE; performance depends on scanner generation and image quality.
citation.notes: High specificity makes a POSITIVE result strongly rule-in (high LR+).

tests.condition: Indirect test for PE via detection of proximal DVT

citation.reference: Patel P, et al. Systematic review and meta-analysis of test accuracy for the diagnosis of suspected pulmonary embolism. Blood Adv. 2020;4(18):4296-4311 (61 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: CUS sensitivity and specificity were 0.49 (95% CI, 0.31-0.66) and 0.96 (95% CI, 0.95-0.98), respectively.
citation.context: Compression ultrasonography detects proximal DVT as a surrogate for PE; low sensitivity means a negative study does NOT exclude PE.
citation.notes: Illustrates a rule-in (not rule-out) test: high specificity, low sensitivity.

tests.condition: Suspected lower-extremity DVT

citation.reference: Bhatt M, et al. Diagnosis of deep vein thrombosis of the lower extremity: a systematic review and meta-analysis of test accuracy. Blood Adv. 2020;4(7):1250-1264 (43 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: For any suspected DVT, the pooled estimates for sensitivity and specificity of proximal compression US were 90.1% (95% confidence interval [CI], 86.5-92.8) and 98.5% (95% CI, 97.6-99.1), respectively.
citation.context: Proximal (not whole-leg) compression ultrasonography for lower-extremity DVT; whole-leg and serial US have different characteristics.
citation.notes: High specificity makes a POSITIVE result a strong rule-in. Verify against source before teaching use.

tests.condition: Rule-out in low / intermediate pre-test probability

citation.reference: Bhatt M, et al. Diagnosis of deep vein thrombosis of the lower extremity: a systematic review and meta-analysis of test accuracy. Blood Adv. 2020;4(7):1250-1264 (43 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: For D-dimer, pooled estimates were 96.1% (95% CI, 92.6-98.0) and 35.7% (95% CI, 29.5-42.4).
citation.context: Quantitative D-dimer for suspected lower-extremity DVT; low specificity means a positive result is non-specific.
citation.notes: High sensitivity / low specificity → strong rule-out when negative.

tests.condition: Suspected heart failure (optimal cutpoint)

citation.reference: Booth RA, et al. Performance of BNP and NT-proBNP for diagnosis of heart failure in primary care patients: a systematic review. Heart Fail Rev. 2014;19(4):439-451.
citation.sourceQuote: Pooled sensitivity and specificity of BNP and NT-proBNP using the lowest [0.85 (sensitivity) and 0.54 (specificity)], optimal (0.80 and 0.61), and manufacturers' (0.74 and 0.67) cutpoints showed good performance for diagnosing HF.
citation.context: Primary-care populations; performance is cutpoint-dependent (values shown are the 'optimal' cutpoint). Specificity is lower and more variable than sensitivity.
citation.notes: Best used as a rule-out (high sensitivity at low thresholds). Acute-care thresholds differ; verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Suspected heart failure (optimal cutpoint)

citation.reference: Booth RA, et al. Performance of BNP and NT-proBNP for diagnosis of heart failure in primary care patients: a systematic review. Heart Fail Rev. 2014;19(4):439-451.
citation.sourceQuote: Similar performance was seen for NT-proBNP: lowest (0.90 and 0.50), optimal (0.86 and 0.58), and manufacturers' (0.82 and 0.58) cutpoints.
citation.context: Primary-care populations; 'optimal' cutpoint shown. No statistically significant difference in accuracy vs BNP.
citation.notes: Rule-out test; threshold- and population-dependent. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: ED patients with suspected ACS without ST-elevation; serial 0h/1h algorithm

citation.reference: Burgos LM, et al. Performance of the ESC 0/1-hour algorithm in the diagnosis of myocardial infarction with high-sensitivity cardiac troponin: systematic review and meta-analysis. Eur Heart J Acute Cardiovasc Care. 2020;10(3):279-286 (11 studies, 19,213 patients).
citation.sourceQuote: Summary sensitivity and specificity in diagnosing AMI were 99% (95% CI 98-99%;63%) and 91% (95% CI 91-92%;96%) respectively.
citation.context: Emergency-department suspected ACS, AMI prevalence ~11%; the 0h/1h SERIAL hs-cTn algorithm, not a single draw. The 63% / 96% after each CI are I-squared heterogeneity, not part of the interval.
citation.notes: Very high sensitivity → excellent rule-out; the algorithm also rules in. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Children with pharyngitis (vs throat culture)

citation.reference: Cohen JF, et al. Rapid antigen detection test for group A streptococcus in children with pharyngitis. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2016;7:CD010502 (105 evaluations, 58,244 participants).
citation.sourceQuote: RADT had a summary sensitivity of 85.6%; 95% confidence interval (CI) 83.3 to 87.6 and a summary specificity of 95.4%; 95% CI 94.5 to 96.2.
citation.context: Children with pharyngitis, RADT vs throat culture; median GAS prevalence ~29.5%. Sensitivity is heterogeneous across studies.
citation.notes: High specificity (rule-in); imperfect sensitivity means a negative RADT may warrant culture in children. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Adults, secondary care (vs duodenal biopsy)

citation.reference: Sheppard AL, et al. Systematic review with meta-analysis: the accuracy of serological tests to support the diagnosis of coeliac disease. Aliment Pharmacol Ther. 2022;55(5):514-527.
citation.sourceQuote: Summary sensitivity and specificity of immunoglobulin A (IgA) anti-tissue transglutaminase were 90.7% (95% confidence interval: 87.3%, 93.2%) and 87.4% (84.4%, 90.0%) in adults (5 studies).
citation.context: Adults in secondary care vs duodenal biopsy; values differ in children (sens ~97.7%, spec ~70.2%). Requires adequate gluten intake and total-IgA sufficiency.
citation.notes: Adult values shown; pediatric characteristics differ. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Known or suspected rheumatoid arthritis

citation.reference: Zhang WC, Wu H, Chen WX. Meta-analysis: diagnostic accuracy of anti-CCP-2 and anti-CCP-3 antibody in rheumatoid arthritis. Clin Chem Lab Med. 2014;52(6):779-790 (17 studies).
citation.context: Anti-CCP-2 assay for known/suspected RA. High specificity, moderate sensitivity.
citation.notes: Classic rule-in test (high specificity); a negative result does not exclude RA. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Suspected influenza A (vs RT-PCR)

citation.reference: Merckx J, et al. Diagnostic accuracy of novel and traditional rapid tests for influenza infection compared with RT-PCR: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Ann Intern Med. 2017;167(6):394-409 (162 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: Pooled sensitivities for detecting influenza A ... were 54.4% (95% credible interval [CrI], 48.9% to 59.8%) for RIDTs ... Pooled specificities were uniformly high (>98%).
citation.context: Traditional RIDT vs RT-PCR, influenza A. Specificity uniformly >98% (0.98 used as the stated floor; no precise spec interval reported). Sensitivity is higher in children and far higher for digital immunoassays and rapid NAATs.
citation.notes: The textbook low-sensitivity / high-specificity test: a POSITIVE rules in, but a NEGATIVE does NOT rule out influenza. Sensitivity CrI 48.9-59.8%. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Suspected PPGL (typically a low-prevalence screened population)

citation.reference: Chen Y, et al. Accuracy of plasma free metanephrines in the diagnosis of pheochromocytoma and paraganglioma: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Endocr Pract. 2017;23(10):1169-1177.
citation.sourceQuote: NMN was generally more accurate than individual tests, with the highest AUC (0.99, 95% CI 0.97-0.99), DOR (443.35, 95% CI 216.9-906.23), and pooled sensitivity (0.97, 95% CI 0.94-0.98) values.
citation.context: Plasma free normetanephrine (NMN) for PPGL; per the same meta-analysis NMN specificity was 0.97 (95% CI 0.92-0.99). LC-MS/MS preferred; cutoff and sampling position drive heterogeneity.
citation.notes: Teaching case: even at ~97% sensitivity AND specificity, the low pre-test probability of PPGL keeps PPV modest. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Suspected SLE (vs systemic rheumatic disease and healthy controls)

citation.reference: Jeong S, et al. Diagnostic value of screening enzyme immunoassays compared to indirect immunofluorescence for anti-nuclear antibodies in patients with systemic rheumatic diseases: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Semin Arthritis Rheum. 2018;48(2):334-342.
citation.sourceQuote: the summary sensitivities of SEIA vs. IIF were 87.4% vs 88.4% for combined SRDs, 89.4% vs. 95.2% for SLE ... the summary specificities of SEIA vs. IIF were 79.7% vs.78.9% for combined SRDs, 89.1% vs. 83.3% for SLE
citation.context: ANA by indirect immunofluorescence (IIF) for SLE. High sensitivity → good rule-out; specificity is limited because ANA is positive in many other conditions and some healthy people. Screening EIA differs (sens 89.4%, spec 89.1%).
citation.notes: Classic rule-out: a negative ANA makes SLE unlikely; a positive ANA is non-specific. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Adults suspected of infection / sepsis (vs blood cultures)

citation.reference: Hoeboer SH, et al. The diagnostic accuracy of procalcitonin for bacteraemia: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Clin Microbiol Infect. 2015;21(5):474-481 (58 studies, 16,514 patients).
citation.sourceQuote: The optimal and most widely used procalcitonin cut-off value was 0.5 ng/mL with a corresponding sensitivity of 76% and specificity of 69%.
citation.context: Procalcitonin at 0.5 ng/mL for bacteraemia (positive blood cultures) in adults; overall SROC AUC 0.79; accuracy varies by subgroup (ICU higher, neutropenic lower).
citation.notes: Moderate sensitivity AND specificity — a good example of a test that only modestly shifts probability. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Adults with presumptive pulmonary TB (vs culture)

citation.reference: Horne DJ, et al. Xpert MTB/RIF and Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra for pulmonary tuberculosis and rifampicin resistance in adults. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2019;6:CD009593 (86 studies, 42,091 participants).
citation.sourceQuote: Xpert MTB/RIF pooled sensitivity and specificity (95% credible Interval (CrI)) were 85% (82% to 88%) and 98% (97% to 98%), (70 studies, 37,237 unselected participants; high-certainty evidence).
citation.context: Adults with presumptive pulmonary TB vs culture. Sensitivity is lower in smear-negative (67%) and HIV-positive (81%) patients, so a negative result does not exclude TB.
citation.notes: High specificity → strong rule-in; imperfect sensitivity (especially smear-negative/HIV+) means negatives need follow-up. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Suspected giant cell (temporal) arteritis (vs temporal artery biopsy)

citation.reference: Ball EL, et al. Role of ultrasonography in the diagnosis of temporal arteritis. Br J Surg. 2010;97(12):1765-1771 (17 studies, 998 patients).
citation.sourceQuote: When the halo sign on duplex imaging was compared with TA biopsy, the sensitivity was 75 (95 per cent confidence interval 67 to 82) per cent and the specificity was 83 (78 to 88) per cent.
citation.context: Halo sign on colour duplex ultrasonography vs temporal artery biopsy. Newer studies and protocols (e.g. axillary involvement, compression sign) may differ.
citation.notes: Imperfect on both axes; a negative scan does not exclude GCA when suspicion is high. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Suspected acute aortic syndrome (rule-out)

citation.reference: Watanabe H, et al. Diagnostic test accuracy of D-dimer for acute aortic syndrome: systematic review and meta-analysis of 22 studies with 5000 subjects. Sci Rep. 2016;6:26893.
citation.sourceQuote: Based on ... 12 studies that used the cutoff value of 500 ng/ml, the sensitivity was 0.952 (95% CI 0.901-0.978), the specificity was 0.604 (95% CI 0.485-0.712).
citation.context: Acute aortic syndrome/dissection, D-dimer cutoff 500 ng/mL. High sensitivity, low specificity. Pre-test probability scoring (e.g. ADD-RS) modifies how it is used.
citation.notes: Rule-out parallel to D-dimer in PE: a value <500 ng/mL substantially lowers the probability of dissection. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Suspected H. pylori, off PPIs/antibiotics (vs histopathology)

citation.reference: Best LMJ, et al. Non-invasive diagnostic tests for Helicobacter pylori infection. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2018;3:CD012080 (101 studies, 11,003 participants).
citation.sourceQuote: The sensitivity (95% CI) estimated at a fixed specificity of 0.90 (median from studies across the four tests), was 0.94 (95% CI 0.89 to 0.97) for urea breath test-C
citation.context: 13C urea breath test vs histopathology; sensitivity estimated at a FIXED specificity of 0.90 (the cross-test median, so no separate specificity CI). Recent PPIs or antibiotics reduce sensitivity.
citation.notes: Sensitivity 95% CI 0.89-0.97. The most accurate non-invasive test; serology and stool antigen are somewhat less accurate. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Differentiating bacterial from aseptic meningitis

citation.reference: Sakushima K, et al. Diagnostic accuracy of cerebrospinal fluid lactate for differentiating bacterial meningitis from aseptic meningitis: a meta-analysis. J Infect. 2011;62(4):255-262 (33 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: The pooled test characteristics of CSF lactate were sensitivity 0.93 (95% CI: 0.89-0.96), specificity 0.96 (95% CI: 0.93-0.98), likelihood ratio positive 22.9 ...
citation.context: CSF lactate to distinguish bacterial from aseptic meningitis, cutoff ~35 mg/dL. CRITICAL caveat: prior antibiotic treatment lowers sensitivity to ~0.49.
citation.notes: Strong test in the untreated patient, but pre-treatment with antibiotics markedly reduces sensitivity. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: ED patients with acute dyspnea (point-of-care)

citation.reference: Al Deeb M, et al. Point-of-care ultrasonography for the diagnosis of acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema in patients presenting with acute dyspnea: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Acad Emerg Med. 2014;21(8):843-852 (7 studies, 1075 patients).
citation.sourceQuote: The sensitivity of US using B-lines to diagnosis ACPE is 94.1% (95% confidence interval [CI] = 81.3% to 98.3%) and the specificity is 92.4% (95% CI = 84.2% to 96.4%).
citation.context: Point-of-care lung ultrasound B-lines for acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema in undifferentiated dyspnea; reference standard was final clinical diagnosis.
citation.notes: Good rule-in and rule-out at the bedside; operator- and protocol-dependent. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Adults with suspected appendicitis (add-on after clinical evaluation)

citation.reference: Rud B, et al. Computed tomography for diagnosis of acute appendicitis in adults. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2019;(11):CD009977 (64 studies, 10,280 participants).
citation.sourceQuote: Summary sensitivity was 0.95 (95% confidence interval (CI) 0.93 to 0.96), and summary specificity was 0.94 (95% CI 0.92 to 0.95).
citation.context: Adults with suspected appendicitis, CT vs surgical/follow-up reference; median prevalence 0.43. IV/contrast-enhanced CT slightly more sensitive than unenhanced.
citation.notes: High on both axes → strong rule-in and rule-out. Verify before teaching use.

sequences.name: DVT: D-dimer → compression US
sequences.stopNote: In low pre-test probability, a NEGATIVE D-dimer typically excludes DVT; a positive D-dimer is followed by compression ultrasonography.
sequences.independenceNote: D-dimer (fibrin turnover) and ultrasonography (direct visualization) probe different biology, so conditional independence is a reasonable teaching approximation.

sequences.name: PE: D-dimer → CTPA
sequences.stopNote: In low/intermediate pre-test probability, a NEGATIVE high-sensitivity D-dimer typically ends the workup (PE excluded). CTPA is pursued when the D-dimer is positive or pre-test probability is high.
sequences.independenceNote: D-dimer (a biomarker of fibrin turnover) and CTPA (direct imaging) probe different biology, so treating them as conditionally independent is a reasonable teaching approximation.
